flashcards.front: What is an AND node in an AND-OR graph?
flashcards.back: An AND node represents a problem decomposition where ALL successor subproblems must be solved for the parent problem to be solved.

flashcards.front: What is an OR node in an AND-OR graph?
flashcards.back: An OR node represents a choice among alternatives. Only ONE successor needs to be solved, similar to standard search.

flashcards.front: What is the solution to an AND-OR graph problem?
flashcards.back: A solution graph (or solution tree), not a single path. It includes one choice for each OR node and all successors for each AND node, with all leaves being solved terminal nodes.

flashcards.front: How does AO* compute the cost of an AND node?
flashcards.back: Cost = sum of [arc cost + h(successor)] for ALL successors. Since all subproblems must be solved, costs are added together.

flashcards.front: How does AO* compute the cost of an OR node?
flashcards.back: Cost = minimum of [arc cost + h(successor)] among all successors. We choose the cheapest alternative.

flashcards.front: When is an AND node marked SOLVED in AO*?
flashcards.back: When ALL of its successor nodes are marked as SOLVED. Every subproblem must be completed.

flashcards.front: When is an OR node marked SOLVED in AO*?
flashcards.back: When ANY one of its successor nodes is marked as SOLVED. Only one alternative path needs to succeed.
The process of transforming a complex problem into a set of easier subproblems using AND decomposition. Each subproblem is solved independently and solutions are combined.

flashcards.front: Name three applications of AND-OR search.
flashcards.back: 1. Theorem proving (decompose theorem into lemmas). 2. Game playing (opponent responses as AND nodes). 3. Planning (decompose complex plans into subplans).

flashcards.front: How does AO* differ from A*?
flashcards.back: A* finds a single optimal path in OR-only graphs. AO* finds an optimal solution graph in AND-OR graphs, handling both problem decomposition (AND) and choice (OR).
